Title: Metabolic crosstalk between hydroxylated monoterpenes and salicylic acid in tomato defense response against bacteria

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S (constitutive) for overexpression; RNAi hairpin construct for silencing
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in leaves upon Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato infection
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Altered in transgenic leaves; reduced in RNAi_MTS1 and constitutively overexpressed in 35S:MTS1
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased MTS1 expression elevates hydroxylated monoterpene emission and promotes stomatal closure but does not enhance bacterial resistance because SA pathway output is reduced; silencing lowers HMTP emission, increases stomatal aperture, and elevates SA-associated defense markers without causing clear susceptibility
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; RNAi silencing; GC-MS monoterpenoid profiling; stomatal aperture measurements; bacterial infection assays; RT-qPCR of PR1 and ICS; MEcPP and SA/GA quantification
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, stomatal closure, intra- and interplant immune communication, contribution to resistance against bacterial infection
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato DC3000 infection; alpha-terpineol treatment; BTH treatment
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overproduction of HMTPs lowers MEcPP, reduces ICS/SA pathway activation, lowers PR1 induction, and can uncouple stomatal closure from effective resistance
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering volatile-mediated defense and plant-to-plant signaling, but should be combined with maintenance or enhancement of SA signaling to avoid negative flux competition
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MTS1 controls emission of key hydroxylated monoterpenes in infected tomato leaves. 2. Higher MTS1 activity promotes stomatal closure and interplant signaling. 3. Excess MTS1-driven monoterpene production reduces MEcPP/SA pathway activation, explaining why resistance is not improved unless SA signaling is restored.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper classifies defense outcome as dependent on metabolic crosstalk between MEP-derived volatile production and SA biosynthesis; MTS1 is treated here as Common Gene per instruction because focus is on volatile output and defense signaling rather than direct enzymatic step characterization.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling by GC-MS; MEcPP quantification by LC-MS/Orbitrap; fluorescence-HPLC for SA/GA; RT-qPCR

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Lower in infected 35S:MTS1 leaves; slightly induced in infected RNAi_MTS1 leaves
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression tracks conversion of SA to gentisic acid and parallels altered SA/GA balance in lines with modified MTS1 expression
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; SA and GA metabolite quantification
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense-associated SA catabolite/derivative during pathogen interaction
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato infection in MTS1 transgenic backgrounds
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reflects altered SA catabolism during defense reprogramming
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. S5H expression changes follow SA/GA metabolic shifts in MTS1-altered plants. 2. Reduced S5H in 35S:MTS1 accompanies lower GA accumulation. 3. Increased S5H in RNAi_MTS1 accompanies higher GA accumulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a primary experimental focus but functionally analyzed in relation to SA derivative balance.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies immediate committed precursor for SA production
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chorismate to isochorismate in the SA biosynthetic pathway
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense hormone required for PR1 induction and resistance to bacterial infection
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher ICS expression associates with higher SA/GA accumulation and defense activation; reduced ICS expression in MTS1-overexpressing or FSM-treated plants lowers SA accumulation and resistance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; correlation with MEcPP quantification; SA/GA measurements; pathogen assays; pharmacological inhibition with fosmidomycin
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced upon Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato infection; reduced in infected 35S:MTS1 and FSM-treated plants
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato infection; fosmidomycin treatment; altered MTS1 expression
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dependence on MEP-derived MEcPP links SA production to plastidial isoprenoid metabolic state
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important target to maintain SA-mediated resistance when engineering terpene metabolism; can be coupled with volatile engineering to avoid defense penalties
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ICS expression is positively associated with MEcPP abundance during bacterial infection. 2. MTS1 overexpression or DXR inhibition decreases ICS expression and SA accumulation. 3. Reduced ICS output compromises PR1 induction and resistance, while restoring SA signaling rescues defense.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Presented as the main SA biosynthetic gene responding to MEP pathway status in tomato.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for MEcPP and downstream hydroxylated monoterpenes; indirectly supports SA biosynthesis via MEcPP-mediated ICS activation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports plastidial volatile defense metabolites and SA-linked immunity through pathway flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Chemical inhibition of DXR with fosmidomycin reduces MEcPP, lowers ICS expression and SA/GA accumulation, and increases bacterial susceptibility
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pharmacological inhibition with fosmidomycin; MEcPP quantification; RT-qPCR of ICS; SA/GA quantification; pathogen assays
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fosmidomycin pretreatment before Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato infection
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Blocking DXR compromises both HMTP production and SA-associated defense, increasing susceptibility
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Highlights need to preserve MEP precursor flux when modifying terpene synthesis for defense traits
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXR activity is required to sustain MEcPP levels in infected tomato leaves. 2. Inhibiting DXR suppresses ICS expression and SA accumulation. 3. Reduced MEP flux leads to higher susceptibility to bacterial infection.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Gene accession not provided; enzyme role inferred from fosmidomycin target explicitly described in paper.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS MEcPP; RT-qPCR; fluorescence-HPLC; pathogen phenotyping

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Reports activation of SA-mediated defense associated with target metabolite levels
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PR1 expression is induced by hydroxylated monoterpene treatments and suppressed in SA-deficient contexts or in MTS1 overexpressors with reduced SA pathway activation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by alpha-terpineol and other HMTP treatments; reduced in infected 35S:MTS1; increased in infected RNAi_MTS1; induced in receiver plants near 35S:MTS1 emitters
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: HMTP treatments; bacterial infection; BTH treatment; interplant exposure
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Marker rather than causal metabolic enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful readout for screening engineered lines with balanced volatile and SA defenses
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HMTPs activate PR1 expression in tomato. 2. PR1 output inversely follows excessive MTS1-driven HMTP production due to reduced SA biosynthesis. 3. BTH restores PR1 induction in 35S:MTS1 plants.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because extensively measured as defense output, though not a pathway enzyme in metabolite synthesis.
